params.topic: M01-L001: Hello and Basic Courtesy — Thai language lesson

presentation_outlines.title: Hello and Basic Courtesy
presentation_outlines.description:
[Title / Opener] Introduce the lesson clearly and set the recording tone.
Lesson focus:
• You can greet people politely, thank others, apologize, and respond yes/no in a friendly first encounter.
• Deliver a 2-line greeting exchange with the correct polite ending.

Layout: lesson-opener. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: The first five seconds of meeting someone in Thailand set the whole tone. With just a few polite words, you can open any conversation warmly. Greet politely, thank and apologise, respond to thanks, and handle simple yes/no in a friendly first encounter.

presentation_outlines.title: What you will learn
presentation_outlines.description:
[Learning Objectives] Make the lesson goals visible before the teaching slides begin.
Objectives:
• You can greet people politely, thank others, apologize, and respond yes/no in a friendly first encounter.
• You can recycle recent vocabulary while using basic affirmation and negation in short controlled output.
• Deliver a 2-line greeting exchange with the correct polite ending.
• Catch greetings, thanks, apologies, and polite particles in slow speech.

Layout: objectives-list. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: You can greet people politely, thank others, apologize, and respond yes/no in a friendly first encounter. You can recycle recent vocabulary while using basic affirmation and negation in short controlled output.

presentation_outlines.title: Your first Thai word: สวัสดี
presentation_outlines.description:
[Teaching Slide] Show สวัสดี as a single large triplet card — the learner's first Thai word.
Key language:
• สวัสดี | sà-wàt-dii | hello
Practice:
• Listen and repeat: สวัสดี sà-wàt-dii. Say it three times.
• Pause-and-produce: you walk into a hotel lobby. What do you say? Say it aloud before I show the answer.

Key vocabulary:
• สวัสดี (sà-wàt-dii) — hello

Layout: focus-card. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: Let's start with the very first word you will ever say in Thai. Listen first: สวัสดี sà-wàt-dii. This one word covers hello, goodbye, good morning, good evening — all of it. สวัสดี is your safe greeting at any time of day.

presentation_outlines.title: Polite endings: ครับ and ค่ะ
presentation_outlines.description:
[Teaching Slide] Show ครับ and ค่ะ as individual cards first, then the combined polite greeting chunks.
Key language:
• ครับ | khráp | polite ending (male)
• ค่ะ | khâ | polite ending (female)
• สวัสดีครับ | sà-wàt-dii khráp | hello (male, polite)
• สวัสดีค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khâ | hello (female, polite)
Practice:
• Listen and repeat: ครับ khráp. Now: ค่ะ khâ. Repeat each three times.
• Substitution drill: I say สวัสดี — you add ครับ. Now I say สวัสดี again — you add ค่ะ.
• Your turn: I point to male or female. Say the correct full greeting: สวัสดีครับ or สวัสดีค่ะ.

Key vocabulary:
• ครับ (khráp) — polite ending (male)
• ค่ะ (khâ) — polite ending (female)
• สวัสดีครับ (sà-wàt-dii khráp) — hello (male, polite)
• สวัสดีค่ะ (sà-wàt-dii khâ) — hello (female, polite)

Layout: contrast-board. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: In Thai, you finish almost every polite sentence with a small word that signals respect. Let's learn two of them now. First: ครับ khráp. Listen: ครับ. This is the male polite ending. If you are a male speaker, you add ครับ after your sentences. Say it with me: ครับ.

presentation_outlines.title: Thank you and sorry: ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ
presentation_outlines.description:
[Teaching Slide] Show ขอบคุณ and ขอโทษ as individual triplet cards, then their polite forms.
Key language:
• ขอบคุณ | khàawp-khun | thank you
• ขอโทษ | khǎaw-thôot | sorry / excuse me
• ขอบคุณครับ | khàawp-khun khráp | thank you (male, polite)
• ขอโทษค่ะ | khǎaw-thôot khâ | sorry (female, polite)
Practice:
• Listen and repeat: ขอบคุณ khàawp-khun. Now: ขอโทษ khǎaw-thôot. Say each three times.
• Discrimination drill: I describe a situation. You choose ขอบคุณ or ขอโทษ — say it aloud. Situation 1: someone helps you carry a bag. Situation 2: you need to pass through a crowd.
• Substitution drill: say ขอบคุณ with ครับ. Now with ค่ะ. Say ขอโทษ with ครับ. Now with ค่ะ.

Key vocabulary:
• ขอบคุณ (khàawp-khun) — thank you
• ขอโทษ (khǎaw-thôot) — sorry / excuse me
• ขอบคุณครับ (khàawp-khun khráp) — thank you (male, polite)
• ขอโทษค่ะ (khǎaw-thôot khâ) — sorry (female, polite)

Layout: focus-card. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: Now two more high-value words. Listen first: ขอบคุณ khàawp-khun. This means thank you. ขอบคุณ. Say it with me: ขอบคุณ. You will use this one constantly — after help, after kindness, after service. Someone holds the door? ขอบคุณ.

presentation_outlines.title: Respond naturally: ไม่เป็นไร, ใช่, ไม่ใช่
presentation_outlines.description:
[Teaching Slide] Show three response words individually, then a mini-exchange pattern demonstrating when to use each one.
Key language:
• ไม่เป็นไร | mâi bpen rai | it is okay / no problem
• ใช่ | châi | yes / that is right
• ไม่ใช่ | mâi châi | no / not right
Practice:
• Listen and repeat: ไม่เป็นไร mâi bpen rai. Say it three times. Now: ใช่ châi. Three times. Now: ไม่ใช่ mâi châi. Three times.
• Response-building drill: I say ขอโทษครับ — what do you say back? Pause and answer. I say ขอบคุณค่ะ — what do you say back?
• Pause-and-produce: is this สวัสดี? ใช่ or ไม่ใช่? Say it aloud before I show the answer.

Key vocabulary:
• ไม่เป็นไร (mâi bpen rai) — it is okay / no problem
• ใช่ (châi) — yes / that is right
• ไม่ใช่ (mâi châi) — no / not right

Layout: dialogue-ladder. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: You can now greet, thank, and apologise. But what happens when someone thanks or apologises to you? You need responses. Let's learn three. First: ไม่เป็นไร mâi bpen rai. Listen: ไม่เป็นไร. This means it is okay, no problem, never mind. Learn it as one whole phrase.

presentation_outlines.title: Pronunciation beat: hearing Thai tones
presentation_outlines.description:
[Teaching Slide] Show tone direction arrows next to the transliteration to visualise pitch movement.
Key language:
• ครับ | khráp | high tone — voice rises sharply
• ค่ะ | khâ | falling tone — starts high, drops down
• ขอบคุณ | khàawp-khun | low tone on khàawp — voice stays low
Practice:
• Tone echo drill: I say ครับ khráp — repeat it. Now I say ค่ะ khâ — repeat it. Feel the difference in your voice.
• Minimal pair choice: I say two words. Which one is the high tone ครับ and which is the falling tone ค่ะ? Point before I tell you.

Key vocabulary:
• ครับ (khráp) — polite ending — high tone

Layout: focus-card. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: Before we roleplay, let's train your ear. Thai is a tone language — the pitch of your voice changes the meaning of a word. Listen to two words you already know. ครับ khráp — that is a high tone. Your voice rises sharply upward. Now: ค่ะ khâ — that is a falling tone. Your voice starts high and drops down.

presentation_outlines.title: Roleplay
presentation_outlines.description:
[Roleplay Practice] Support the roleplay recording with clean turn-taking.
Dialogue:
• You | สวัสดีครับ | sà-wàt-dii khráp | Hello.
• Staff | สวัสดีค่ะ | sà-wàt-dii khâ | Hello.
• You | ขอโทษครับ ใช่ไหมครับ | khǎaw-thôot khráp châi mái khráp | Excuse me, is this right?
• Staff | ใช่ค่ะ | châi khâ | Yes.
• You | ขอบคุณครับ | khàawp-khun khráp | Thank you.
• Staff | ไม่เป็นไรค่ะ | mâi bpen rai khâ | No problem.

Key vocabulary:
• สวัสดีครับ (sà-wàt-dii khráp) — Hello.
• สวัสดีค่ะ (sà-wàt-dii khâ) — Hello.
• ขอโทษครับ ใช่ไหมครับ (khǎaw-thôot khráp châi mái khráp) — Excuse me, is this right?
• ใช่ค่ะ (châi khâ) — Yes.
• ขอบคุณครับ (khàawp-khun khráp) — Thank you.
• ไม่เป็นไรค่ะ (mâi bpen rai khâ) — No problem.
• สวัสดีครับ (sà-wàt-dii khráp) — Goodbye.
• สวัสดีค่ะ (sà-wàt-dii khâ) — Goodbye.

Layout: roleplay-dialogue. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: You approach a reception desk at a hotel in Bangkok. Greet the staff, confirm something, thank them, and handle a small exchange politely. You | สวัสดีครับ | sà-wàt-dii khráp | Hello.

presentation_outlines.title: Recap
presentation_outlines.description:
[Lesson Recap] Finish with a simple, memorable checklist.
What you can now do:
• How do you say hello politely as a male speaker? Say it aloud.
• Someone helps you. What do you say? Add a polite ending.
• Someone apologises to you. What is the warm Thai response?
• What is the difference between ใช่ and ไม่เป็นไร?
• Say sorry politely as a female speaker.

Layout: recap-checklist. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: How do you say hello politely as a male speaker? Say it aloud. Someone helps you. What do you say? Add a polite ending.

presentation_outlines.title: Closing
presentation_outlines.description:
[Closing / Sign-off] Give Nine a clean final slide to close the recording.
Takeaway:
• By the end, you can greet, thank, apologise, respond to thanks, and answer yes or no politely.
• Next: First Contact and Courtesy continues.

Layout: lesson-closing. Use Noto Sans Thai Looped for all Thai and transliteration text. Use Sarabun for English text.
Use a clean, solid-colour background. Do NOT add any images, illustrations, stock photos, textures, or background patterns.
IMPORTANT: Keep the right third of the slide completely empty — it is reserved for a camera overlay (PiP zone).

Speaker notes: By the end, you can greet, thank, apologise, respond to thanks, and answer yes or no politely. Next: First Contact and Courtesy continues.

elements.expectedText: 1. Say hello politely with a correct polite ending
2. Use ขอบคุณ (khàawp-khun) and ขอโทษ (khǎaw-thôot) in the right social moments
3. Distinguish ครับ (khráp), ค่ะ (khâ), and คะ (khá) at a practical level
4. Answer with ใช่ (châi) or ไม่ใช่ (mâi châi) and soften with ไม่เป็นไร (mâi bpen rai)
5. Deliver a short first-contact exchange with politeness

This is your safe, polite greeting for a first meeting.
Treat politeness as part of the whole line, not as an extra decoration.
Learn the greeting as a full social line, not just the base word.
สวัสดีครับ (sà-wàt-dii khráp)
สวัสดีค่ะ (sà-wàt-dii khâ)
ไม่เป็นไร (mâi bpen rai)
Smooths the moment after thanks or a small apology.
Each phrase has a social job in the interaction:
After help, service, or a small kindness
ไม่เป็นไร (mâi bpen rai)
Answers a thank-you or small apology
Thai polite endings do real work - they shape the tone of the whole line.

khráp  -  male polite ending
Use for statements and questions

khâ  -  female statement ending
Use for statements and answers

khá  -  female question ending
Use when asking questions
Beginner rule: learn ครับ (khráp) and ค่ะ (khâ) first for safe polite statements.
Say each line with the correct polite ending:
Add ครับ (khráp) or ค่ะ (khâ)
Add ครับ (khráp) or ค่ะ (khâ)
Add ครับ (khráp) or ค่ะ (khâ)
Add ครับ (khráp) or ค่ะ (khâ)
5. ไม่เป็นไร___ (mâi bpen rai ___)
Add ครับ (khráp) or ค่ะ (khâ)
Answer yes, no, and no problem
Three response tools - two answer the idea, one softens the moment.
Softens the situation - not a yes/no answer
At a reception desk - practise the full exchange
ขอโทษครับ สวัสดีครับ (khǎaw-thôot khráp sà-wàt-dii khráp)
สวัสดีค่ะ (sà-wàt-dii khâ)
นี่ใช่ไหมครับ (nîi châi mái khráp)
ขอบคุณครับ (khàawp-khun khráp)
ไม่เป็นไรค่ะ (mâi bpen rai khâ)
Look away and try: how do you say hello politely as a male speaker? Say it before looking back.
What is the difference between ขอบคุณ (khàawp-khun) and ขอโทษ (khǎaw-thôot)? One closes warmly, one repairs politely.
Someone says ขอโทษ (khǎaw-thôot) to you. What do you say back? ไม่เป็นไร (mâi bpen rai).
What do ครับ (khráp) and ค่ะ (khâ) do? They are the polite endings that tell the listener how your line should land.
Practise the full exchange - greeting to thank-you to no-problem

You can now greet, thank,
apologise, and respond politely.
You have the smallest courtesy toolkit that already works.
Next lesson: names and countries - introducing yourself.
